_authoring_notes: v3 first-pass rubric (no prior v2 on main). Authored via the scaleout author↔Gemini-critic loop under the strict-provenance bar: every scored goal must trace to a verbatim or paraphrased user message (instruction.md, user_simulation_prompt.md, or oracle_intents.json). Oracle-specific identifiers the user never voiced are stripped unless the user pinned them verbatim. Weights reflect user emphasis, not test_manifest.yaml F2P gate weights.

_provenance.goal_0: N/A — anti-gaming guard, derived from the harbor benchmark's defensive floor (no positive weight, can only zero the trial). Not a user ask.
_provenance.goal_1: Turn 5 verbatim: 'we should document this keyword in @packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md @packages/coding-agent/README.md ...' is the explicit documentation request; Turns 0/1 ('tell me how that works and what packages come up', 'so we can't search just by keywords?') supply the context for WHAT must be documented — the keyword-based npm discovery mechanic. The golden patch answers exactly this by documenting the registry search (curl '.../v1/search?text=keywords:pi-package') in the coding-agent docs. This discoverability purpose is co-central to the documentation deliverable. The keyword token itself is scored separately in goal_2; this goal scores that the keyword's discovery/search purpose is documented, accepting either a runnable search command (the golden's form) or equivalent prose so it does not over-pin the golden's exact syntax or require the search mechanic verbatim.
_provenance.goal_2: Turn 2 verbatim: 'we should ensure that we have a unique keyword we can search for, how about pi-package?' — the user named the exact keyword. The golden documents exactly this: 'Published pi packages should include the `pi-package` keyword in their `package.json`'. 'pi-package' is named verbatim by the user, so naming it here is permitted. Establishing the keyword convention in the docs is co-central with the search mechanic (goal_1); together they are the documentation deliverable the golden delivers.
_provenance.goal_3: Turn 5 verbatim: 'we should document this keyword in @packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md @packages/coding-agent/README.md and any other .md file that talks about pi packages.' The user named both files verbatim and the golden patch edits exactly these two (README.md and docs/extensions.md). Files named verbatim, so allowed. This goal scores the breadth/placement requirement (both named files), distinct from goal_1 (the search mechanic) and goal_2 (the keyword token) which score WHAT is documented; this scores WHERE.
_provenance.goal_4: Turn 3 verbatim: 'no, add it to ../pi-doom/ ../pi-package-test/ and ../pi-gitlab-duo/' and Turn 4 'then publish new versions of these'. This is a direct, substantive later-turn user command and must be scored (criterion D / full multi-turn coverage). It is weighted as a real secondary (0.20), below the documentation core, because the user's headline ask was exploratory/documentation and the golden/reference solution delivers the convention via docs rather than manifest edits (the user-sim trigger table flags the named sibling dirs may be absent in the container and require graceful handling). It is deliberately NOT a high-weight core — the prior rubric's defect was weighting manifest edits 0.50 + a version bump, which scored the documentation-only golden just 0.25. At 0.20 the documentation-only golden still scores 0.80 (>=0.7) while the genuine Turn-3 ask is meaningfully rewarded. The publish step (Turn 4, 'then publish new versions of these') is subsumed here: the npm-publish network action cannot complete in-sandbox, but its durable, verifiable workspace artifact is a bumped `version` field in the same edited manifests, which this goal rewards alongside the keyword add. Either target path (named siblings or in-tree substitution) earns full credit.

_dropped_from_v2.note: No v2 rubric existed on main for this task. This is the first-pass rubric authored at the v3 bar via the scaleout author↔critic loop.

_added_for_coverage.note: Not applicable — no v2 baseline to diff against. Per-goal user-ask tracing lives in each goal's rationale and in _provenance.

goal_1: weight=0.4, tier=core, user-turn cite=yes — provenance OK.

Gemini critic final: verdict=PASS score=1.0 round=8.
strength: Excellent handling of the captured-tree artifact in goal_0, ensuring sincere edits aren't wrongly rejected.
strength: Smart separation of keyword breadth (goal_1) from the specific keyword choice (goal_2).
strength: Perfect behavioral proxy for the 'publish' ask (goal_4) by scoring the version bump.
residual risk: None identified; the rubric is exceptionally robust and handles edge cases (like missing sibling directories and captured-tree artifacts) perfectly.

_task_quality_flags.internet_with_live_origin: task.toml sets allow_internet=true. goal_0 anti-gaming guard names git-ops exfiltration as a zeroing trigger where applicable.

completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine documentation/discovery deliverable that edits real, tracked, in-tree files in the workspace. It is NOT a no-op or gamed artifact. Reject if ANY of these bad patterns are present: (a) a zero-edit diff, or a diff whose only content is a captured-tree banner such as '=== repo/ ===' rather than real file changes; (b) git-ops exfiltration or state-resetting such as 'git checkout main', 'git reset --hard origin/...', or reverting tracked files instead of editing them; (c) a comment-only or whitespace-only diff that adds no functional documentation or keyword content; (d) changes confined to temporary or scratch locations clearly not the real package source (e.g. /tmp, *.bak, throwaway copies) rather than the real tracked markdown and manifest files; (e) the keyword or doc prose present only inside a string literal in test/verifier code, a commented-out block, or a duplicate of unrelated existing text. NOTE: sincerely editing user-named sibling packages (pi-doom, pi-package-test, pi-gitlab-duo) when they are present in the capture is a legitimate way to follow the user's later asks and is NOT gaming, even though those siblings live outside the pi-mono package tree; capture banners like '=== pi-doom/ ===' are diff-capture artifacts and must not be mistaken for content.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor (names >=2 concrete bad patterns: zero-edit/capture-banner-only, git-ops exfiltration, comment/whitespace-only, throwaway scratch files, string-literal-in-test-only). The golden patch is documentation-only (edits two tracked markdown files), so the floor rejects only genuinely gamed artifacts, never a sincere doc or keyword edit. Flagged, not scored.

completeness_goals.goal: The project documentation explains that the unique keyword exists so pi packages can be discovered/searched for on the npm registry — i.e. it documents the keyword's discoverability PURPOSE the user asked to understand. The load-bearing requirement is that the added prose makes clear the shared keyword is the handle by which a reader finds pi packages on npm. Showing a concrete runnable search example (for instance a `curl` against the npm registry search endpoint `.../-/v1/search?text=keywords:<keyword>`, or an equivalent `npm search keywords:<keyword>` invocation) is the form the golden uses and fully satisfies this, but it is OPTIONAL: a clear prose explanation that the keyword is there so users can search/discover the packages on npm — even without a verbatim CLI command or exact search-mechanic syntax — equally satisfies the goal. It must be real documentation prose newly inserted into tracked markdown, not a placeholder, a commented-out block, a string in test code, or a duplicate of unrelated existing text.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 5 verbatim: 'we should document this keyword in @packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md @packages/coding-agent/README.md ...' is the explicit documentation request; Turns 0/1 ('tell me how that works and what packages come up', 'so we can't search just by keywords?') supply the context for WHAT must be documented — the keyword-based npm discovery mechanic. The golden patch answers exactly this by documenting the registry search (curl '.../v1/search?text=keywords:pi-package') in the coding-agent docs. This discoverability purpose is co-central to the documentation deliverable. The keyword token itself is scored separately in goal_2; this goal scores that the keyword's discovery/search purpose is documented, accepting either a runnable search command (the golden's form) or equivalent prose so it does not over-pin the golden's exact syntax or require the search mechanic verbatim.

completeness_goals.goal: The documentation establishes a single, unique discovery keyword convention — the specific token the user proposed, 'pi-package' — as the shared handle that pi packages should carry so they can be found by the npm keyword search. The doc must (a) name 'pi-package' as the convention's keyword and (b) describe it as a keyword that published pi packages should include in their `package.json` (e.g., in the `keywords` array) for discoverability. It is the user-agreed unique token, not a different ad-hoc/generic token and not merely the pre-existing generic 'pi' keyword. The convention text must be genuine documentation prose, not a stub, a comment, or a literal in test code.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 2 verbatim: 'we should ensure that we have a unique keyword we can search for, how about pi-package?' — the user named the exact keyword. The golden documents exactly this: 'Published pi packages should include the `pi-package` keyword in their `package.json`'. 'pi-package' is named verbatim by the user, so naming it here is permitted. Establishing the keyword convention in the docs is co-central with the search mechanic (goal_1); together they are the documentation deliverable the golden delivers.

completeness_goals.goal: The keyword-discovery guidance is recorded in BOTH coding-agent documentation files the user named: `packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md` AND `packages/coding-agent/README.md`. Each named file gains new prose covering the keyword convention and/or the search mechanic. The load-bearing requirement is that both explicitly named files are updated with real, newly inserted documentation about the 'pi-package' discovery convention — not a placeholder, a commented-out block, or a duplicate of unrelated existing text. The user's open-ended 'and any other .md file that talks about pi packages' clause is optional credit-worthy elaboration, not a hard requirement, since only these two files were named explicitly.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 5 verbatim: 'we should document this keyword in @packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md @packages/coding-agent/README.md and any other .md file that talks about pi packages.' The user named both files verbatim and the golden patch edits exactly these two (README.md and docs/extensions.md). Files named verbatim, so allowed. This goal scores the breadth/placement requirement (both named files), distinct from goal_1 (the search mechanic) and goal_2 (the keyword token) which score WHAT is documented; this scores WHERE.

completeness_goals.goal: The shared 'pi-package' keyword is applied to one or more real, tracked package.json `keywords` arrays — such as the user-named sibling packages (pi-doom, pi-package-test, pi-gitlab-duo) when they are present in the capture, or the available in-tree pi-mono packages when the named siblings are absent (editing both is also acceptable) — preserving any pre-existing keywords if present (i.e., adding the new keyword without deleting existing entries). This makes the documented convention concrete by putting the keyword on actual manifests so the keyword search can find them. As the durable artifact of the user's follow-up 'publish new versions of these' (Turn 4), additionally bumping the `version` field in the same edited manifests to a higher value is an optional but credit-worthy elaboration; the keyword addition alone is sufficient for full credit on this goal. Every path fully satisfies the goal: there is no penalty for following the named siblings, no penalty for the graceful in-tree substitution when the externals are absent, and no penalty for editing both. The keyword must be a genuine array entry in a real manifest, not a hardcoded value in test code, a commented-out line, a throwaway scratch file, or an entry added only to a private package.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 3 verbatim: 'no, add it to ../pi-doom/ ../pi-package-test/ and ../pi-gitlab-duo/' and Turn 4 'then publish new versions of these'. This is a direct, substantive later-turn user command and must be scored (criterion D / full multi-turn coverage). It is weighted as a real secondary (0.20), below the documentation core, because the user's headline ask was exploratory/documentation and the golden/reference solution delivers the convention via docs rather than manifest edits (the user-sim trigger table flags the named sibling dirs may be absent in the container and require graceful handling). It is deliberately NOT a high-weight core — the prior rubric's defect was weighting manifest edits 0.50 + a version bump, which scored the documentation-only golden just 0.25. At 0.20 the documentation-only golden still scores 0.80 (>=0.7) while the genuine Turn-3 ask is meaningfully rewarded. The publish step (Turn 4, 'then publish new versions of these') is subsumed here: the npm-publish network action cannot complete in-sandbox, but its durable, verifiable workspace artifact is a bumped `version` field in the same edited manifests, which this goal rewards alongside the keyword add. Either target path (named siblings or in-tree substitution) earns full credit.

decomposition_notes: The user's session is fundamentally EXPLORATORY/DOCUMENTATION: Turn 0 asks how npm registry keyword search works and what packages surface; Turn 1 probes keyword-only search; the durable deliverable the golden/reference patch produces is documentation in two coding-agent markdown files explaining (a) the 'pi-package' keyword convention and (b) the registry search command (curl '.../-/v1/search?text=keywords:pi-package'). The DOMINANT weighted core therefore lives in DOCUMENTATION, matching the golden: goal_1 (keyword-as-discovery mechanic, documented, 0.40) + goal_2 (the unique 'pi-package' keyword convention, 0.25) + goal_3 (both user-named doc files updated, 0.15) = 0.80 of the weight, all delivered by the golden. Turn 2 fixes the unique keyword 'pi-package' (goal_2). Turn 5 is the explicit documentation request and names the two doc files verbatim; the golden edits exactly those (goal_3) and Turns 0/1 supply the context for what is documented (goal_1); the 'any other .md file' clause is optional elaboration subsumed under goal_3. Turns 3 and 4 ('add it to ../pi-doom/ ../pi-package-test/ ../pi-gitlab-duo/' then 'publish new versions') are real, substantive multi-turn follow-ups and are scored as goal_4 (0.20, important) — adding the keyword to real manifests, accepting either the user-named siblings (when present) or graceful in-tree substitution (when absent, per the user-sim trigger table). goal_4 is deliberately a 0.20 secondary, NOT a high-weight core: the golden/reference solution delivers the convention via DOCS rather than manifest edits, and weighting manifest edits as core (the prior rubric's defect: 0.50 add + version bump) scored the documentation-only golden just 0.25. At 0.20 the genuine Turn-3 ask is meaningfully rewarded (criterion D) while the documentation-only golden still scores 0.80 (>=0.7). The publish step (Turn 4) has no durable in-sandbox artifact beyond an optional version bump on the same manifests and is subsumed under goal_4. Turn 6 ('search') is an ephemeral verification action whose only durable workspace artifact is the documented search mechanic (goal_1) and the keyword on manifests (goal_4). Turn 7 ('commit an dpush') is process/VCS and excluded per rubric rules. goal_0 is the anti-gaming floor (weight 0.0). Weighted goals (goal_1/2/3/4) sum to 1.00 and rank by user importance (documenting the search mechanic + keyword convention dominates, manifest edits secondary); this does NOT mirror the test_manifest F2P split, which inverts the emphasis — putting 0.60 of its weight on keyword-add-to-manifest gates (t4 added/breadth/critical) that the documentation-only golden never satisfies, and only 0.25 on the doc gates.
